description: The master list of the smallholder coconut farmers who supply Betrimex across the Mekong Delta. It anchors supplier analytics and is the endpoint of every traceability query — answering which farms a product came from and how much of supply is organic-certified.
Stable identifier used to link a farmer to their deliveries and to finished product.
The supplier's ID in source systems, used when reconciling with field/procurement records.
Identifies the individual grower for audits, payments, and farmer engagement.
Shows where supply originates, supporting regional sourcing strategy and risk concentration.
Province-level grouping for geographic supply reporting.
Confirms a farm's certification status — the basis for organic sourcing and premium pricing.
Fast filter to size organic vs. conventional supply for EU/USDA audit scope.
The catalog of Cocoxim finished-goods SKUs. It lets the business slice production and sales by product and by product family, and distinguish organic from conventional lines.
Stable identifier linking a SKU to its production runs and sales orders.
The branded product as customers order it — the level at which pricing and demand are managed.
Rolls SKUs into categories for portfolio-level revenue and yield comparison.
Separates the premium organic range from conventional for margin and compliance analysis.

The manufacturing footprint — each processing plant and the lines within it. It enables plant- and line-level performance benchmarking (yield, throughput, downtime) and separates organic-certified facilities from conventional ones.
Stable identifier linking a specific line to its production runs.
The site responsible for output — the unit for capacity and plant-vs-plant comparison.
The specific line, used to pinpoint where yield or quality issues arise.
Flags organic-dedicated facilities, which matters for certification segregation.

Who Betrimex sells to — domestic distributors and international export buyers. It underpins market analysis: revenue by country, region, and channel, and the domestic-vs-export split that drives commercial strategy.
Stable identifier linking a customer to their orders.
The account being sold to — the level for relationship and account management.
Destination market, key for export reporting and trade/tariff analysis.
Groups markets for territory-level sales performance.
Route to market, used to compare domestic, export, and retail economics.
The headline split between home-market and export revenue.

The shared calendar that ties intake, production, quality, and sales to a common time axis. It powers all trend, seasonality, and period-over-period reporting (harvest seasons, monthly yield, quarterly revenue).
The join key every fact uses to report "by date".
The actual day an event occurred.
Year-over-year comparison of supply, output, and sales.
Quarterly business reviews and targets.
Monthly trending and ordering of periods.
Readable month labels for reports and dashboards.
Daily granularity for operational analysis.
Reveals weekday vs. weekend operating patterns.
Weekly operational and supply cadence.
Separates weekend operations, which affect shift staffing and throughput.

The catalog of quality-assurance checks Betrimex runs and the specifications each must meet. It defines what in-spec means and lets quality results be grouped by test category for food-safety and compliance reporting.
Stable identifier linking a test definition to its results.
The specific check performed, e.g. sweetness or bacterial load.
Groups tests for category-level pass/fail rollups (esp. food safety).
The ideal result the product should hit.
Defines the pass/fail window around target.
Makes results interpretable and comparable to spec.

Every batch of coconuts received from a farmer at a collection point — the raw-material supply that feeds production. It measures how much is coming in, at what quality, and from where, and is the starting point for supply traceability.
The traceable unit of incoming supply, referenced all the way to finished product.
Ties each delivery to the farm that supplied it.
Enables intake volume and quality trends over time (harvest seasons).
When supply arrived — used for freshness and seasonality analysis.
Identifies the receiving hub for logistics and regional intake reporting.
Geographic origin of supply for sourcing and risk analysis.
The core supply-volume measure (tonnes of coconut received).
Key quality measure that drives downstream processing yield.
Intake quality classification used for pricing and sorting.
Identifies certified-organic supply for segregation and audits.
Quick at-a-glance status to spot problem lots (low sweetness/grade).

Each batch processed on a plant line — the heart of manufacturing performance. It links raw input to finished output and carries the headline efficiency KPI (yield %), along with the quality and downtime signals that explain why yield moves.
The unit of production, linking inputs (lots) to outputs (product) and tests.
Enables production and yield trending over time.
Ties output to the SKU being made.
Attributes performance to a specific plant and line.
Where the batch ran — for plant-level benchmarking.
Pinpoints the line for performance and troubleshooting.
What was made in the run.
Compares performance across shifts (staffing/operating patterns).
Marks when processing began, for scheduling and duration analysis.
Marks completion, for throughput and turnaround analysis.
How long the batch took — a throughput/efficiency measure.
Raw material consumed — the denominator behind conversion efficiency.
The target output given input and recipe — the yield benchmark.
What was actually produced — the basis for efficiency and loss.
The headline manufacturing KPI — how efficiently coconuts convert to product.
Quantifies lost output — the gap to target in absolute terms.
Buckets runs into performance tiers for quick exception spotting.
Input-quality signal — a leading explanation for yield variance.
Input-quality signal — higher Grade A typically lifts yield.
Lost operating time — a key driver of throughput and OEE.
Flags organic production for certification and segregation.
Indicates supply blending and the breadth of traceability per run.

The food-safety and quality record — every lab/line test performed on a production run. It evidences that product met specification, supports pass/fail reporting, and is central to compliance and customer assurance.
The unique record of a single quality check, for audit trails.
Ties each test to the batch it certifies.
Links the result to its spec definition and category.
Enables quality trend and pass-rate analysis over time.
The check performed (e.g. sweetness, bacterial load).
Groups results for category-level food-safety rollups.
The actual result — compared against spec to judge quality.
The benchmark the result is judged against.
Defines the in-spec window for the result.
Makes the value interpretable against spec.
The compliance outcome — the core quality KPI.
Timing of the check, for in-process vs. final QA analysis.

The commercial outcome — what was sold, to whom, and at what value and margin. It is the revenue and profitability backbone, and via run_id it links every sale back to the batch and farms that produced it.
The unit of sale — the grain for revenue and fulfillment reporting.
Enables sales and revenue trending over time.
Attributes revenue to the SKU sold.
Attributes revenue to the customer/market.
The link that makes a sold product traceable back to its source.
When the sale occurred — for revenue timing and seasonality.
The buying account.
Destination market for export reporting.
Territory grouping for sales performance.
Route to market for channel economics.
The home-vs-export revenue split.
Sizes the premium organic business.
The product on the order line.
Sales volume measure.
Pricing measure for price realization analysis.
The primary revenue measure (reporting currency).
Revenue in the transaction currency for local reconciliation.
Identifies the transaction currency.
Profitability rate — a core commercial KPI.
Absolute profit contribution of the order.
Service-level KPI for fulfillment reliability.

The traceability backbone that records which farmer lots went into which production run. It resolves the many-to-many between supply and production and is what makes farm-to-product (and product-to-farm) tracing possible for certification audits.
The production batch side of the supply-to-production link.
The incoming-supply side of the link — the lot consumed.
Carries the source farm so traceability resolves in one hop.
How much of each lot fed the batch — supports mass-balance and contribution analysis.
